speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Hershiel Tribble recalls labor conditions and foreign workers in potlatch logging camps around World War I and some misgivings about the IWWs. He explains the tricks of log scaling and some early forms of entertainment such as medicine shows, courtship, and moonshining.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Pat Malone like as you remember him?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh,

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: he was an old Irishman and he was a city cop there for a long time. He liked his booze and nobody... He was just people he could trust, you know. Some of the moonshiners, they were thick during probation days and he never seemed to arrest the main people there. Somehow or other they seemed to get by, but once in a while some stranger came in and started from a poor way or put out poor moonshine or something. He was a good old guy, everybody liked him. People that knew him really liked him. He kept pretty good order and I think his popularity helped him to get by more than anything else. Nobody wanted to harm him and they didn't want to get in bed with him either. He was a very accommodating old fellow and he was pretty well liked. I remember when my wife and I came back from Spokane on my honeymoon and old Pat, he congratulated us and said, the first boy, the first one was a boy, he said, call it Pat. Of course, we didn't. I wasn't Irish enough hardly myself to call it Pat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that he didn't do very much really most of the time. It seems like he had so many friends that in a way that was a good way to keep law and order.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I think so. I think it helped him to get by I believe. If he hadn't been liked, if he'd been thoroughly disliked, he'd have had a hard time. But, men he was so well liked, if anybody did try to turn against him now there's too many friends that would hold up for him. I think that's how he held his job and why he gave satisfaction as much as he did. They just laughed at his mistakes and things that he didn't see purposely. I heard he was the butt of more stories than probably anybody around both sides. Oh, there's always making jokes about him. I remember one of these medicine shows came in there one time and I guess they did a little inquiring beforehand maybe, but anyway a couple of them got there in front like they do, you know, telling jokes. One of them asked if he knew what an island was and he said no. Well he says it's the city marshal surrounded by kegaboos.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Things like that, you know, there's always rigging up something. He's good natured, you know, and as long as it isn't a joke, old Pat was in for it. It suited him all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did carnivals come through very often? Did what? Did you say it was a carnival that came? Carnival?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh, well one of these medicine shows, you know. They come in and they put on a little free show generally and then they sell their medicine and usually they want to pick out some popular girl in town and they want to sell medicine and so many chances of voting for it and she's supposed to get a real good prize or so much money or something and of course that helps to sell the medicine. They get a girl that's pretty popular and her folks want her to win it and others, some of their best friends want her to win it and each one of them tries to outdo the other and they buy medicine that they never needed in the first place and wouldn't have did them any good probably if they did. The kind of medicine they usually got, but anyways it's good for the show. It has quite a hand. I used to do that in Palouse too. Lots of places. In those days they traveled around from little town to little town and they prayed on the public. They'd probably get an old pair of scissors out there and cut some glass and say if you do that and your wife can't cut cloth or whatever, they'd show them and give it a few wets on the whetstone and then show them how to cut this glass and just cut it all up as nice as it please. I remember I was a kid and I got home one time and I was going to try cutting some glass. I didn't know that could be done with scissors. I took some scissors out and tried to cut the glass and I couldn't cut it. I don't know what kind of scissors they had or what kind of glass it was, but I sure couldn't cut anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like they were entertainment for the people. Oh yes.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I remember one time when I came in Palouse's there and it was supposed to be the Great Kamama. I remember his name yet. Some guy would get up and introduce somebody and then somebody else would introduce him and find out that he'd introduced the Great Kamama and he'd come out there with a big old robe on him. He looked like some fellow from India or some place. He would heal things. They had a liniment that they would rub on. I thought you could even cut your arm off your leg without feeling it. They'd do anything with that liniment. They'd just rub it on and have somebody come up there and cut a wart off or something and take it off. He'd say he didn't feel it. They'd go for that. They'd pass people out around in there to sell this liniment. Because we was foolish enough to get some, we were foolish enough to buy one of those stones. We just liked the other people. At home we couldn't sharpen anything with the stone. We couldn't cut any warts off the liniment without feeling it either. It was a joke, them shows, but they seemed like they could buy with that stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If they just kept finding new suckers?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yeah, they'd go from town to town. They'd find some new gadget to sell. I've got a gadget up there that I've had for years that didn't even unwrap. I'd give it to anybody. I took one and looked at it after I bought it and knew it wasn't no good when I looked at it. So I didn't even try it. I never got around to it very many times. I couldn't afford it. But once in a while they put up an unusually big lie about it. Maybe they'd take me. But I don't think as many crooks those days there is now. The ones now are better educated and smoother about it. If they have to be, they get by. The people would catch on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other kinds of entertainment that were a little more above board? What was it like to be in the house parties?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, those days, of course, they had little house parties. They'd have house sweats or dances. They didn't even charge. Some of the neighbors would furnish a little music. They didn't care what kind it was. It was the noise. They didn't care what the floors were like those days. They didn't break a leg. They'd follow through cracks. They'd do things like that. They'd have regular old parties sometimes. This is old spin the fight and post office and all these things. Kids' games, maybe even grown people. They didn't care. Things like that for amusement. It was jolly in those days. They had to put in their time somewhere. Most of it was working. They'd have these parties and little house sweats, they called them. Various things. They used to, many years ago in these country plays, they'd have a school called Literary and Debate. That was really something quite nice. I remember a few of them. I never took part in them, but I was to a few of them. They'd have a program, whatever they could. If somebody could sing, they could sing. Anything they could do that would entertain the public. All free, of course. Then they'd have a debate, they called it. They'd choose up sides. I can't remember too many of the things, but one of them I remember was whether a horse was more valuable. If it was resolved that a horse was more valuable than a cow or vice versa, something like that, one side would take the cow and the other side would take the horse. They'd get up there and put on quite an argument. Then they'd have some judges. They'd judge which side won. Quite a lot of comedy. They'd get on some pretty funny things, just describing them people, depending on how witty they were. Oh, another result that a woman had an easier time in life than man. All sorts of things. Anything they could think of. Them together with a little entertainment, it was real nice. Christmas way. And all the women would string popcorn and things like that. Great strings of it. Yards and yards of it. They'd have the whole tree decorated with this. Then after presents were taken off and everything, why, the kids would be grabbing these great strings of popcorn and eat. All sorts of things as that. They'd have little programs, you know, as to go along with it. All sorts of things as that. There's no cars, no way to go anyplace hardly, just horses. So they made their own amusement and things. They thought they had a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would a couple go courting if you were interested in a girl?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, they spent quite a bit of time at the houses, with the girls' house, quite a bit of the time. And they'd go for walks and things like that and come back and linger on the gates. They usually had yard fence and gate. And they'd lean on that and talk and things like that. They'd go for buggy rides lots of times. Horses and buggies, you know, that was quite a run. The boys would go to see his girls on a horse and buggy. And they'd go for maybe one horse or maybe two horses and buggy they had in both ways. And they'd go for a drive and back or they'd go someplace on Sunday to visit or various things. Some of her girlfriends or his boyfriends or something like that. Or other couples. And that's the way they did most of the courting then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be likely for a person to court with more, you know, the number of different people before they found the right person?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Nothing like it is now. No where near like it is now. They did, of course, there'd be some particular people to do that. But quite often the girl would marry the first man that she ever went with. They'd go together maybe quite a long time or something. But quite often she would marry the first one she'd ever went to and quite often men would marry the first one she'd ever went with. But then there'd be others that wouldn't marry for quite a while. There was quite a few who didn't marry. But nothing like it is now. And they took marriage seriously those days. Of course, shipping all these things. They got married. They figured that was it. They settled down to work and make a living and raising a family and all those things. I think that the modern cars and everything, transportation, the phones, the radio and televisions and all that, they wrecked all of those things. There's so many different ways now that they can entertain or young people can go to. They couldn't even do any of those things anymore. Nobody would come to them anymore. They'd try to have a literary debate or anything like that. People would look at them or laugh at them and think that was just a kid's show. But those were nice in those days. So I don't know. They all had a good time. I think they had a good time those days as they do now. They worked harder, had less, didn't have any money. I think they enjoyed life. It was just about the same thing. They had the same feelings as people have now. They didn't have the same things to enjoy but they didn't know it because they didn't know there was such a thing. There wasn't such a thing at that time so they didn't miss them. Now they're taking away from a person. They know what they're missing but those days they didn't know nothing about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would a couple figure when they were going to get married and the plans that went with that? Would they be figuring it when they were going to be setting up housekeeping and their own different home? Would they often stay with the husband's family or the wife's family? When would they decide to get married after this courtship of however long?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, the courtships, I think usually there was a nearly always a courtship those days. Not just a meeting of a few hours or something and then the decision to get married. There'd be a courtship nearly always. For some length it might vary anywhere from just a short courtship up to a month or even years. But then they made the decision why they didn't, very seldom any big elaborate wedding. Not so quite often they just married justice of peace or just a ministry in a small church in a small gathering, friends and relatives who put family there a little bit and things that way. They'd set up their own housekeeping pretty quick as a room. Sometimes they might stay at their folks if they had plenty of room to stay there. Maybe they'd even work like a boy for his father. He's a big farmer, maybe he'd stay there and his wife and they'd have a place to stay or maybe a separate little house there or something. But they'd quite often try to buy themselves a home or rent a place to live but they usually went to themselves when they were married. They didn't very often stay at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was getting married considered the end of being a kid? When you got married did that mean you were grown up?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Pretty much so, yes. They didn't go out and play after they got married. Many of them. They settled down and the woman with the house worked. She'd probably start having children pretty quick. She'd have her hands full in those days because they had to work hard and long hours for a living. And while the women, when I was a young fella, the women didn't work out at all. They'd work out unless it was a woman who was her husband and she had to work out for a living or support a child or two. But it was just almost untodded for a woman. The woman couldn't even get a job those days if they tried and they didn't think the woman should. They thought it was the man's place to make a living and support him. A man figured that. He figured if he couldn't make a living for a woman he didn't have any business marrying her in those days. He'd been horrified at the thoughts of his wife getting out and working to help him to support him. Although from what I hear it sounds as if the woman was working about as hard as the man. I think this is what women live with. I think they already had it before. I think they're losing it with what they're doing because before they were kind of looked upon as a kind of a queen or something. They never had to go out from the house to do any real hard work. Of course they had plenty of hard work in the house. I don't mean a woman didn't work hard. But I mean if they didn't do any heavy work hard, the man was usually in the house. He had no furniture or anything heavy to move. He'd help them do that. But he wouldn't hardly wash dishes or make beds or anything that way. But the man would go out and work and make a living. He was supposed to do that and support him and stay there. The woman she had plenty to do. Maybe she'd help with a little garden and things of that way. By the time she cooked for the men and raised her children and done her housework, she didn't really have any time to go out and work out. I'd heard that quite a few women did farm work with the men working up in Bouville working in the timber that women were expected to take care of the fields pretty much. Well, I tell you, I think a lot of this women working, I think a lot of that started with the world wars pretty much. Because the men were overseas and there was a shortage of labor. And they went out and helped in that and started in taking men's work over. But they never got to a man's pay at that time. That's what started all this commotion about equal rights and things. I think myself, if a woman does it, when a man is working exactly at the same place, he gets the same price. I think that part of it. But, I don't know, I had a lot of respect for women. I guess I put them up on a pedestal. I thought they were to be looked up to. It was the man who was supposed to support them and take care of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you handle your fields and your farming when you were working?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Exchange work mostly. When I was young, I was putting all the exchange work in. I didn't have any money to hire anybody with. And each farmer would help the other and they'd take and, you see they used a hay baler. When they built hay then it took a crew of about seven to run one of these horse -powered balers. And they'd go from place to place. And the farmer fed them at each place. Whenever they liked, if you wanted an outfit there at your place, your wife would get dinner for them. One of the neighbor wives would come in and help her. When the crowd was there, she'd get the meal for them, whatever meals they were going to have while they were there. And you'd bail for me next while they'd come there and we'd furnish the food. And that's the way they went until it was done. But there was hardly any money paid. Now the young fellas didn't have any farm or any exchange. Some people liked that, they'd have to. But the wages were very, very small in those days. It seemed we were thrashing, you see. Now when thrashing, I forget, I think the crew, if I remember right, was about twelve or fourteen of these on the thrash machine. And even in the early days, that's the way they did it too. The family would feed them wherever they, whoever they thrashed for, the French board would feed them. And they would move from place to place. But there was very little hiring done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a hard time getting your work done while you were in Bovill? Well, you mean at home?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, meaning the farm and whatever farm you did. Well, I did very little farming. You see, when I was a young fellow, we bought this land from the company, the potlatch lumber company, with a contract to log it. And we logged it to the Palouse River, and then the spring when the water was high, we drove it to Potlatch. And that's where we'd make a little money on that. And we had practically no farmland, maybe just a little bit on a cow. In my case, and lots of other cases would be the same, my wife was here, why she'd do the milking, in fact she could milk a lot better than I could. And when our children got older, they'd done milking and things like that and helped. And that's how we'd get by on those. Of course, the bigger farmers, some of them didn't even go to the woods, they figured they were making a living on their farm and cattle and such as that. But those of us that were down in the low payback area, that's the way we saw it managed that way. It was a stump ranch, so we didn't have to pay rent, and the care owner nights and Sundays were the only time we had, and work all during the week. It would get a little bit clear, it would take us years to do it, but at least if we stayed with it about ten years, we had about ten year contracts on these stump ranches, it didn't cost as much anyway in the first place. For forty acres, about the best they had here would cost you about eight hundred dollars for forty acres. But that was a large bill for a full of those days, you know, if you didn't have any money. But we'd work on that, and the payments were so small, they were probably just as small as rent would be, and at the end of ten years, as we stayed with it, we'd own the place, whereas with paying rent we would own nothing. That's the way we looked at it, not as an investment, but as a place to live cheaply. And we'd go on that way to work, our family didn't used to go with us, we'd go away to work, maybe only come home, I worked in camp there when I only got home about once a month. Most of that was before I was married, but after I was married, most of the time I'd get home Saturday night, until Sunday night. My brother -in -law, he had an old Dodge car at that time, and he and I used to ride back and forth the other week. So I thought I was pretty lucky then, I got home about one day a week. But we never moved any place. We went to Bovin one time, and my wife cooked for us for three months up to the rest of the story, that's the only time my wife ever went to the same place as we were. She always stayed home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a special contract that you were doing right around there at that time that you were all up in Bovill?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, we were working for the Potlatch lumber company, which was the apartment there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, at the time that she was cooking for you?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, we were working for them at that time, yes, but most of them were boarded in camp, and that's where we'd have boarded if we hadn't had my wife with us. But we rented a little house up there about four miles from Bovill, a little place they call Collins, and she stayed in that. She took a yellow jaundice just before one of our babies was born, and the baby was born premature at seven months, and we had an awful hard time of saving him. And we came down where we didn't go back to the woods then, that, we didn't want to have to stay up there for a while, and my wife and I couldn't go back right then anyway for a while. So we stayed down here for a while. Then eventually they got logging down here, and she worked down here, handy then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you think back on the old days in Bovill, you hear about logging towns as being really rough towns, and we're talking about Pat Malone, which makes me think about that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was a pretty rough town, Bovill?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, not for a lumber town. I don't believe that Bovill was a rough town for a lumber town. There was once in a while a fight there, and something like that, but not serious. There was no murders, no even guns, which ultimately were used or anything, maybe just a fight between lumberjacks or something. You didn't hear of robbery and theft and crimes committed there at that time, and it was about anything. Lots of times quarrels of course, but they had, of course a lot of this time I was up there doing prohibition. That might have made a difference too I suppose. I think all the time I worked at Bovill I believe it was during prohibition days. So that held the trouble down, but there was very little trouble up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did that hold the trouble down?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I think lack of liquor for one thing, of course they had the moonshine, but they had to be a little bit more careful with that. And I think that the people up there, most of the people were putting on all lumberjacks for the loggers. And I tell you the truth, I think they were too busy working to fight, I think too tired maybe. You know if people are real busy and they don't have time to get into trouble so much, I think that had a lot to do with it. I think they were just too busy to fight, and too tired. And there were quite a few of them foreigners, I don't think they could hardly argue with one another, they couldn't understand one another, a lot of C -J's.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No I don't think it was too bad a town at all. I think it was exceptionally good down at the, for a backwoods town, I've been in others when I went on hunting trips and things like Pierce and some of them places where it was terrible. And trouble and drunken people around over the streets and things, I don't remember seeing much drunkenness out in the streets, no doubt there was lots of times that I didn't see, but I don't remember seeing much of it at Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would make Bovill different from some of the other backwoods towns?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I've been there since probation was ended, and they never had much in the line of liquor at Bovill like a lot of these little places have. Not near as many beer pouters and things. And they all seemed to be pretty well operated and pretty quiet, reasonably quiet. I just don't know for sure, the only thing I can say is I went one term of school and I was there a year at that time. And I worked several years up there in the woods, and it had been pretty much what I should have seen some going on, I didn't see it with very little of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know any stories about the old bootlegging and prohibition days, any stories about Pat Malone and the way he was, what he was doing?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, not too many, he was used as a joke on a lot of it. I know he used to be around there and he knew everybody in town, of course he knew everybody there, the regulars, he knew them all well. And they were pretty well established, most of those people were regulars who worked there, engineers and conductors and such as that on trains and camp foremen and things like that with their families were pretty good people, they were not troublesome people. That's one of the reasons why there wasn't much more trouble there. But once in a while there'd be something go on, I know that there was a man who came to camp there, there was one time he came in, he was a Frenchman and he was all dolled up beyond his means. And he was working the same salary I got, so I know he didn't have very much money, he had a family in Saint Maries there to support a wife and two children, two daughters. And he got to courting another woman in Bovill to show you, T .P. Jones, I'll just tell you what his morals was, he got courting a woman there in Bovill and T .P. Jones found out and boy, he went out and fired him just right now. He wouldn't stand for nothing like that if he knew it at all, as a superintendent of the woods department, see, and a man working for him, another man, he got in trouble with a girl and I don't know, there was going to be a lawsuit over it and he was working in camp to try to get some money to fight in this lawsuit. And T .P. Jones went out and made the fullman fire him, he wouldn't even let him work in camp, so the morals of T .P. Jones was just the very highest and he was the superintendent there so he had a lot to say and a lot to do about it. And I think Pat Malone's morals was high too, except he didn't think it was wrong to have a little drink once in a while. Well, I heard Dick said that T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones himself didn't mind the man drinking.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh, I don't think that he cared too much about it, he knew that some of that was going on in town, he had to know it, he was too smart a man to let that go by. But I think that T .P. Jones figured as long as there was decency and they would behave themselves, I think he just tried not to say it. Just ignored it, I don't think he did that, but the immorality or anything like that, T .P. Jones didn't have anybody mixed up with him, if he knew it he'd fire him if he was working for him. He wouldn't stand for anything that way, I think his morals were very high, except for like I said, he just figured, I think, he ignored it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the men were pretty aware of the way T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones felt personally? Did he make that known to the men?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the men pretty naturally just...

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh, I think they knew it by experience, I don't think he would make it known to them, I think they just noticed that he ignored it. I'm talking about the morals. Oh, the morals. Well, I think that probably those that knew him real well, and if the case came up and they were talking about it, they'd probably tell other people and everything, I think it would be pretty well known that an established his morals would be. And he had, I think he never had, but Bob Jones, he never had but one child, a girl, I don't know of any children T .P. had, I don't know if he ever had any children or not. But Bob, his brother, he was a camp foreman all the time, Arthur, he had one daughter.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And then there was Jake Jones, a brother to him, a section boss, he had a son, that's all the children I knew of him, and I don't think that Jones had hardly any children. The family was very small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that most people, most of the lagers, did drink during Prohibition?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I think the majority of them would drink some. Very few of them were drunkers though. Very few, because there was no drunkenness. I don't remember any drunkenness in camp during working hours, I think maybe a time or two, maybe over a weekend it'd be a little, but very, very little. It wasn't always too easy to get moonshine in those days, but they did go out, oh they'd be still set up someplace out in the timber a few miles off if they could get there. Once in a while they'd manage to go and get some liquor, but the foremen wouldn't put up with it at all themselves, you know, they'd fire them, they didn't dare let men go out in the works drunk, and they'd fire them if they tried to go out drunk. But I think for the conditions there, I think everything was managed quite well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know any guys that had stills?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, yes I knew two or three of them that had stills.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: They were pretty good citizens too. If a person had a still, if they would have wanted to have operated it right, I think they could have operated a long, long time. They never got caught, I think they would have did that any place, but they don't do it. Every time anybody moonshines, he wants the money right away. In most cases they're lazy people doing all the work for a living anyway, and they have to have the money, they can't go without the money. And they just make a little bit and they sell it, and they get a good friend, they trust him, pretty soon he introduces other friends, and pretty soon they're caught. But if that fellow would take and make a lot of it, never sell a bit of it, make a lot of it, and then dispose if he's still buried or destroyed or anything, and take and get one big wholesale customer there, and just so that he could trust her, whether he couldn't have any witness or anything, and just turn the whole thing over for him, and wash his hands, I believe that fellow could have operated a long time. He never got caught, because it would have been awful hard to have caught a man like that unless they caught him with his steel or something like that and went on to him. I know a man that operated up the creek here, he had several different places where he had steel up here, some rocks up here about three or four miles above my place, and he got in among them rocks someplace and got in among them holes dug out, and he put up a stovepipe, and then took tamarack bark, and put around this stovepipe so it looked like a tamarack tree, or not a tamarack, I mean a cedar tree, and it looked just like a cedar tree, and of course if he had it still going light the smoke wouldn't be noticeable anyway going up among the trees, that's how he operated and kept out of sight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did certain guys have a reputation for making real good moonshines, as opposed to others?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes they did, and some people they claimed was making poison, I don't know if they called it that, I guess it was all poison enough if you drank too much it would probably, I don't know. There was a lot of that around this country here when they was logging down here, but there still wasn't any drunkenness in the camps and things, but other people, most of the drunkenness was around advances or places that didn't work in the camps. Most drunkards are not holding down a good steady job, I don't know if people lay around and don't work anymore they can help, is the rule. Moonshiners were the same class, that's the reason they were doing it because they didn't want to work.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I guess there was a lot of gambling too in the camps. Well there was gambling some in some of the camps, I don't know the camps I worked in, one night there was a newcomer, Ok, there was a new fellow that came in, he did some gambling and one night there was a fellow come around in the office there with a foreman I was and said that the fellow had pulled a gun on him. So we started in there but in the meantime he ran into the car joint and jumped out and ran for the woods. So he didn't stop for anything, he had a pack sack there, took it in the office and there was shells for his pistol there and shells for it and some of his personal belongings. Sometime later the foreman got a letter from him from Saint Maries and he wanted to send the pack sack back up, he threatened him if he didn't but he never paid any attention, he never got his pack sack back anyway. And they gambled, some of them would gamble all night quietly, nobody paid any attention to them. One fellow he seemed to win pretty regular every time I went to Bovill, he'd send some money in for me to deposit it in the bank in his name and he seemed to be doing alright. Some of the fellows were winning, of course they had to be a bunch of losers, there were so many winners, they did quite a lot of gambling alright but nobody paid any attention to that as long as they did their work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know if we got it on the other side but you were saying that in the evening the foreman would just not go through the camp.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, the foreman he didn't pay any attention, he left in the privacy of the camp life in the evenings and Sundays and times like that, he didn't bother them and if it was too rainy, which is very seldom, they worked in rain usually, if it wasn't too bad, they'd gamble some then but they were allowed that privilege, that was about the only thing they did for pass away the time or anything, they were allowed that privilege. As long as they did their work, the foreman didn't know nothing about the rest of us.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And then we started talking about the Balkan people, the Austrians and the... Oh yeah, there was only a few white people in the camp, that is English speaking, pure English speaking people that lived around, there was the foreman and clerk and usually a cook and usually the donkey punchers or skinners they called them, the donkey engines. And sometimes hook tenders, the man that was in charge of the donkey, the donkey would be in charge of the riggin slingers, the man that worked on that donkey called him a hook tender. The rest of them were all foreigners, about every country represented, quite a lot of Austrians, Hungarians and the people from, oh a few from Russia, not very many from Russia, Czechoslovakians and Finns, Bulgarians and just about everybody you could think of and there's quite a mixture. But they all seemed to get along pretty good, hardly ever any trouble among them, some of them couldn't understand the others of course and maybe that helped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're describing this language to me that most of them could understand.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, it was a, they called it a Slovenian language, it seems as though it was sort of a universal language among just a few countries there that each one could understand it enough to talk with one another. And they'd be talking around, I know every place I went somebody would say something about Dobra, Dobra I couldn't understand what Dobra was, it finally got on my nerves a little bit and I thought I had to find out what that was. So it turned out that it'd be about like good in our English language, if it was going by in this quittin time they'd say Dobra, we'd say quittin time, maybe they'd say Dobra meaning that was good, the day was finished. Or if you had an ax why, examine it and maybe Dobra secret, secret was ax and Dobra was good, they had a good ax, a good sharp ax or anything like that they used it for so many many things there and I found out how Dobra meant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what were some of the other words?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I had one sentence I learned there to put together, it was just for the fun of the thing, it was Daimi Molly Schultz Piva. Daimi was to give me and Molly was small and Schultz was let's see a glass or a bottle or something and Piva was beer. Willicke was big in that language and I don't know, I knew quite a few words there for a while, I couldn't talk it but they had to say some English words that there was no word in their language that they could translate. So they'd put in a few English words and then the few words that I knew I could tell the general run of the conversation but I couldn't tell too much about it nor talk with them but I wouldn't know what they were talking about. That's as far as I got, I never really tried to learn the language.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could most of them speak English pretty good after they'd been there for a while?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Most of them could, yes, most of them could. Very few of them there that couldn't understand it at all, some not very well maybe but most of the fellas were real good in English. They, of course they was all other people on the opposite side of war, a lot of them you know with us and while they were real good hearted people they just, you could borrow money from them if you wanted or anything, I never did borrow money from them once. I'm a fellow not knowing any of them and they'd offer to them a good try to sell them bonds because as clerk I had tried to sell the bonds and they wouldn't buy them at all. They'd all offer me money, if you want money you get the money, they'd offer me but no bonds and that's quite a kick but I liked them and the fellas would actually offer well behaved and awfully nice. I borrowed money from a fella, I don't know whether they got the name, I borrowed money down here to camp one time, I needed some money right back for just a little bit, he was from Switzerland and a real nice fella and I needed a few dollars and I asked him if he lent me fifty dollars and he said sure, he just pulled it out of his pocket. He handed it to me and I told him I wanted to pay it back, pay it back and so on a pay day I went to pay it back and he wasn't there and I asked what happened to him and he said he went back to the old country. It made me feel pretty bad and they said he'd be back though and so eventually he did come back in one or two and I asked him why he didn't let me know and I told him I didn't even have any money at the time and he said oh yeah, he wasn't worrying about it anyway. And they was like that, them kind of people, that's the only time I ever borrowed any money from but it shows you the kind of people they were though. If they liked you, well you didn't have no interest or no security or no nothing, that's what you asked them for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much tension with these Europeans who were on the other side in the war?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, there wasn't, not at all, I didn't notice it one bit, of course they might have figured that they'd get in trouble pretty easy if they said anything or started anything at all, they'd be in real trouble here and they didn't feel safe and that, but I'll say one thing, they did disappear and a bunch of people never returned. I can't hint, there's a lot of them I like real well, they were just awful nice people but I don't know, they just disappeared when that happened, I suppose they thought they'd better get back to the old country and maybe they were deported for all I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean after we got into the war?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, after we got into the war, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they all disappear at once? Was it all pretty close together in time?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I don't know too much of course, you see when I went and enlisted in the navy myself, I would have been drafted if I hadn't, I didn't want to have been drafted. And of course after the war was over and when I went back to camp, it was quite a lot different then, there was hardly any of those people there anymore, none that I knew, and they just pretty much disappeared and it seemed like from then on, people living around took over. And they furnished about all the wood crews in locally and around the country, oh Spokane, they had a employment office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they pretty clannish or was there an awful lot of mixing between the different groups?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I wouldn't call them too clannish, I have seen foreigners sometimes that were, especially the Italians when they were working on the railroads and stuff, of course they couldn't talk English language, a very good lot of them. But no I didn't think these people were clannish, they would seem to be very sociable, very friendly, and in the bunk cars, because I used to go out there sometimes, it was kind of fun to go out and talk to them. And in there they'd always be real nice to me, they'd start conversations, they'd talk with me all the time when I went out through there, and I think Dick found the same thing, the same too if anybody went in. I think maybe they kind of liked to talk to people if they were natives, I don't know, but I'd go in there and there wouldn't seem to be any quarrelling or there wouldn't be just groups, you know, some separate groups that was around. I didn't notice any conditions, all hardly among them. But things were pretty tough in the camp then in the early days when I first went in there. See, like I said, there was hardly any white people in there, but when I first went there, I was real hard up for a job, there wasn't very many jobs around, especially a young inexperienced person. And when I went there, there were no electric lights, there was no hot and cold water, cold water but no hot water, there was no place to take a bath. And there, them bunk houses, they had held these people, they weren't long, kind of sort of boxcars, same as a boxcar, 40 feet long, I think the standard length, well 41 feet I think standard length for a flat car, boxcar maybe. But it was in there, there was the bunks and in the middle, they had the old, what did they call them, box heaters. They built a sort of a fence around them, a little board maybe about six inches high, out from the sides and further out past the two ends and they filled this with sand for protection from colds I suppose. And these people, most of them never took a bath, there was no place for them to bathe unless they went in a nice little creek.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Once in a while, some of them were a little bit neater, they'd get in a five gallon can and punch a few small holes in the bottom of the tree and jump in under it right quick, fill it with water and jump in under it right quick and try to take a shower from that. But anyway, of a night there were socks, they had these big old wool heavy socks on and they'd be wet from sweat, if they weren't wet from the snow and water they'd be wet from sweat. They'd hang these on wires above the stove to dry. Some of them used tobacco, chewed tobacco and they'd spit at this sand around the stove and a lot of them weren't very good shots. They might either aim too low or too high and hit the stove or the floor. And then with these socks stewing up above the stove, you can think that there must have been a little odor in there. In fact if you wasn't used to that and didn't have a Castile stomach, I don't know if you'd ever make it full length, that's god save your last meal if you'd ate or not. I tell you that was wicked. And then some Italians in there too, if they could just get a hold of a little garlic that really finished it then. Boy I tell you that cow was terrible. And that's one reason why these IWWs had a little gripe coming was things like that. And now there's no place to take a bath. Of course those of us who lived at home would get home once in a while or maybe get into town or something but it was tough for any of us. Then they finally, after these IWW trouble they had and the big meetings began and one thing or another and a lot of them walked out and they shut the camp down. And that's why they put in a little delco plant system at camp and electric lights and they put in a shower bath there. They didn't cover the top, just a little overhead. And a fellow being there taking a shower and then they had some wash tubs, bowls right there rejoining that. And some fellow being in the shot just gives you water about the right temperature and somebody take a cold bucket of water out of them and leave it over the top. And he'd come out and everybody would be real innocent when they came out and he didn't know who had done it. That used to be one of the favorite jokes in there. But now these old lamps, they had kerosene lamps at that time when I first went to camp. And they had a bull cook around there. He was supposed to start the fires of the morning and he was supposed to keep these lamps filled with oil and wash the chimneys if they got too dirty. They usually wasn't too dirty he didn't think and you can imagine the amount of lights you'd get in the kerosene lights around there. It was a pretty tough place now at one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear many complaints when you were first there about these conditions?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: They had to pack their own beds. They had a lot of graybacks. And when the people come home and bring some people up here and work a little bit in camp and come back with graybacks. They had to fight them when they got home. They had it quite a way though. I heard a fellow tell how to get rid of them things. He said you put a lot of salt on your clothes and he said them graybacks would eat it and he said you go down close to the water, jerk off your clothes and lay them down. The graybacks would all run for a drink and he said then you grab your clothes and run while they were down getting a drink. He said you could get rid of them every time that way. I never tried. I never had the graybacks thank goodness.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: But it was pretty rough those days but then after they got in there and fixed things up like electric lights and places to lay and things that certainly helped them out a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the IWW did have to do with that? I mean as far as the changing and improvement of the conditions goes?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: They helped a lot because it showed so much dissension. It showed a lot and other people began to realize that things were awful bad and even the employers. They were being showed up to the public in every place all over the United States the conditions that people were living under in the woods and the workers. They did help a lot but it took the foreigners to do it. Those people didn't even have naturalization papers here in this country. I wouldn't join it and a lot of us wouldn't join it that lived here. We weren't going to join an organization that's going to tell us how to live in our own country. Of course we needed somebody to tell us how to live I guess but we liked for our own people to do it. But there was no, they weren't too mean. I'd go out nights and come back and everything on there. I never had anybody that tried to get mean with me. They talked to me and tell me the conditions as they seen it in camp and think I ought to leave but I had a lot of doctors all the time paying doctor bills for and drug bills. I had a hard time of it and I had to work just every minute I possibly could. I told them I came there of my own choice. That they didn't get me the job or get me there and I told them I was going to leave at my own choice. But we'd leave in the good nature and none of them ever got mean to me about it or anything that way at all. I thought I should walk out with them while I didn't belong to them and had nothing to do with it so I felt differently. But there's no question that they did have quite a bit to do with helping improve us. We had a big meeting there at Spokane and sent delegates up our centers. We picked our best spokesman and they got up and talked and gave good talks to people. We picked him and after this they gave everybody a chance to talk and oh boy did they make a mess of things. I didn't even feel sorry for my own race there after some of them got up and talked. That's the reason they picked somebody that could talk sensible and it wasn't even reasonable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were a 4L representative and they were meeting with the IWWs. They were meeting with those, yes. And you're saying that the IWWs were the ones that weren't making too much sense.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well actually yes they were the ones that weren't making much sense. But some of the 4Ls, the belief in that was to get together and both sides to get together and talk things over and that was what this meeting attempted. But it just got wild out of order. You could see what some of the people would do when they were grouped and allowed to. They just took over almost everything there and one fellow jumped up and he started to, you better take this down. You better take this down. And he started rattling away and he said never mind, he said, there's a word you're saying that's being taken down. He said don't you worry. Well just things like that and each fellow he thought that he was saying things that the other fellows had left out that should be said. Hauling his ideas out and I don't know, disgusted. You felt as though you'd lost all you'd gained. I had a feeling that when I left that place there that the working man had lost on that meeting. If I'd been a judge I'd have said that he did it. He lost out heavy just by his own ignorance. And those fellows didn't talk sensible or anything. It was radical. They wanted this, they wanted that and everything else. When they tried to get me to quit they'd tell them about the Filton camp, how dirty the cooks was and things. I don't know, I'd been in two or three camps and I'd never found it that way. My brother was a cook and in one and I know that man wouldn't put anything on the table, he wouldn't eat himself. And there was no filth there and he was very particular about that camp. Well I said well maybe that camp isn't but some of the others, well I didn't know about the others of course. I imagine some camps maybe they were dirty, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that the four L's were better, had a more reasonable approach at this meeting? Were they better?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well they talked sensible, see. They were sensible people, most of them were maybe better educated than the others and they were able to talk reasonably. The others just go radical, you couldn't even talk with them and say yeah there was no reasoning to them. So I don't know how two sides can hardly get together that way. If one side's reasonable and the other one isn't even less than reasonable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember a little bit of what the difference was, what the four L's were saying and what the IWW was saying?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: The four L's didn't say a heck of a lot, they were just asking to find out their complaints and everything to try to deal with them, see. And see what they could meet and what they were to make an agreement. But they just got up there and they demanded everything. They had wages way up, they had to have I don't know everything in camp just modern and up to date and they had to have everything they knew. If they'd had intelligence at all, them fellas if it was a demand and that stuff, they thought they couldn't possibly get all them things, they'd have got reasonable and talked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how do you reason with anybody like that?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: That's the thing that got my goat, it made me feel ashamed of the working man, you know, to think that you get up there and spoil everything. They were spoiling everything, see. You can't get any sympathy on your side if you're just going to go clear out a reason with everything and everybody knows they couldn't do that for you if they wanted to, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most of these guys were guys that just didn't have, were they mostly foreigners, did they mostly have little education or who were the kinds of guys that were doing this?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I think it was pushed by the ones that didn't have education. Mostly they had it in for the big guy, the moneyed man and things. Well, I sure not got it in for a money man, if he earned his money honestly, it's his money. And if he doesn't beat people out of it, you know, and he gets it honestly, but they just, them fellas had their way, they'd take everything everybody's got, there'd be nobody to even furnish any big things for them to work at or to invite people. Nobody had money or anything. But, I don't know, their demands were just out of reason. The 4Ls, of course I know what someone told me, well, they don't do anything, that's a company outfit. Well, perhaps so, but there was the idea, the company outfit, and the other outfit, let them talk together, see. That's the way I see it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let them get together, let them put in their complaints and let the company outfit, of course those of us up there, we just let them work in men like the others, wasn't I?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Like I was a clerk in camp and I was appointed secretary of the 4L there. Well, I was just the same as them fellas that were striking in a way, I mean I had the same interests they had, but I don't know, I didn't think they got into it, I mean they undoubtedly did, that undoubtedly helped a lot, see, but I ain't gonna sit for some reason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did that meeting take place, as so far as the changes and conditions around here, was this before the conditions had improved?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, that was before the conditions had improved, that's right. Conditions were bad at the time that happened, but I can't tell you the date of it now, but it was, I think, like the first world war, I don't know, maybe 1919 or 20, somewhere in there, 2012, in that area, someplace in there, I think, probably catch it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, to go back to the beginnings of this labor activity, if you think about when it started, do you remember a real change from when the men began to talk about changing conditions?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that they didn't earlier, that there wasn't a lot of complaining, and then I get the idea that suddenly there was.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I expect maybe among these foreigners, they probably were doing complaining all the time, among themselves in languages that I wouldn't hear, probably, and then the bigger places were, they were getting organized, you see, other than the camp, and the bigger places, that's probably where the planning and most of that was being, the brood, you know, was probably come on. And so, I don't know too much, and then there was a little blank there, I was home a while, I was in the Navy a little while, and that was during some of the hot parts of it, too. But the meeting in Spokane that I was sent as a delegate to was after that, after I came back, of course. But they had a strike there, and everybody walked out, there were just 13 men left in the camp I was in, I imagine there was around 100 that camp usually had, pretty near 100 anyway, and they just had enough to run one donkey and some men had to do double duty, they'd go out in the woods and maybe bring in some log and then come in and sew them up and load them and things like that, that's all they was able to do for a little while at that camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which camp was this, and was this the only camp that was struck?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, no, all the camps were struck. They had quite a shutdown and all of them, I was trying to think, I was at five quite a while and I was at camp two quite a while.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I believe it was what they call camp five. It was out in Feather Creek country there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did it go on?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roughly.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I don't really know, it didn't go on too long there.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: You see, it wasn't just the complaints altogether, I don't think it was the complaints at this meeting and things, but the company couldn't get men, see? It was shutdown and run so short -handed, I think that had about as much to do with improving conditions as the strike did, well the strike of course was responsible for that, but I mean as the complaints did for the men, see?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I have a feeling that they figured they were going to be forced to improve things, or to fire up enough. So they actually improved rather rapidly, see, they changed things over there, it wasn't very long until they furnished the bedding, and then honored the bedding too, you see? And they put in this light plant and hot and cold water for them and things like that, and put in liquid lights. And all this was in a fairly short period of time in there, when they started to work them over, see?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And then after that, well there wasn't much dissension in camp then, from then on that I know of, I have, oh, you know there's radicals and people that holler about this and holler about that and complain, but as far as any general disturbances from complaints over there, I didn't hear much from then on. People seem to be fairly well satisfied.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This tape isn't just about, oh, I'd rather stop too soon than too late. Well, I think we have a little bit of tape left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the men do when they went on strike and they weren't working? Did they still stay and live in the camps?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, no, they left camp. Now, the camp was in, there was a little station out there they called Collins, it's just a little small place where you're setting out of the storm, if you're waiting for the train, see, it's on the Milwaukee tracks. And this camp was out there, I don't know, I remember a mile or two from there, and I know these people there, there's about two of them stayed there, come there and take us to this little station to talk to people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody left camp for town or went in the camp and went by there, see?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And so they'd try to talk to everybody to discourage them from working and from going back into camp and such, but I don't know where all they did go, they just left camp and disappeared. As far as I'm concerned, I don't know where they stayed at or where they went to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wondered if many might have camped out there.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: There were no camps established around near there that I knew, no place that I knew of, no, and they wouldn't allow them to accumulate in town if they had to, I'd have known that too, there were very many people that accumulated in town. But I don't know, they might have a lot of them went to Spokane, but of course I don't know what they'd do without money, they wouldn't have much money, I don't think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many what you called white people also IWWs, or were they mostly foreigners?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: They were all foreigners.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I think about the only people that really were English, English speaking people that really belonged in this country would be more likely to be an officer in that, where he thought he could get some money out of her, something like that. I would say rather than them, because they were the common laborers, them people were striking mostly, you see, your foremen and donkey engineers and all them kind of people, they had a higher salary, and railroaders and things, they had a higher salary. But the railroad, of course the railroad strike was different, they had a railroad strike there once too, but that wasn't the IWWs, the railroad struck, I think that was some railroad organization. They struck, and then them logging trains, they just put green horns on them, they'd never been on a train before for conductors and brickmen, they had to have a time there for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember about when the rail strike was, was that in the same period as these logging strikes?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: It was a little bit different, I think it was a little bit later than them if I remember right, I don't remember too much, I never did any work on the railroad myself at all. While there in camp I know we had a lot of fun out of it, in a way they'd get in front of the first crew, just grab the green horns, didn't know anything about it, I think maybe they had the engineers in the engines or something that had been very dangerous, maybe a man or so here and there, but they'd give them all kinds of motions. They wouldn't back up like you'd get out here and motion somebody who didn't have any signals at all, they'd just motion their hand in any direction, they couldn't even understand them or anything. They had to be awful careful, it was dangerous for a while, but of course they soon caught on, where it wasn't quite so bad, then some of the men came back. That strike didn't last too awful long, but some of these fellows went to the coast and got to work, they never did come back, the old timers did, of course I think they figured they probably would get in bad anyway. But then some of the better ones did eventually come back and get back on. But they'd soon train them for that, it was just woodwork, you know, mostly getting logs out of the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it related to the IWW strikes, do you think, was it the same kind of thing?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I don't know if that helped to stir it up or not, it might have given the ideas or something that way, and got strike ideas in their head or something, I really don't know too much about the railroad strike, just what happened on that. But I do know that they, for just a little while there, it was quite a mess. But they were determined, they were always going to go out and try to show these fellows they get along without them, so they'd just grab them and get them and take them on the railroads. And that gave them a chance too, they got on the data, they learned a little bit, you see. So most of them, I don't think they gained much on the strike. I don't know if they gained anything or not. They might or they might not, but I really don't know what the result was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was there so much shortage in the local people out in the logging camps when you first started out there?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, settlements were small, people hadn't been used to that. They lived down there, there was nobody living up there hardly at all. In the first starter, they got that little town of Bovill started, but most of those people were railroad men, you know, that operated trains and things like that, and mains and machinery, there were people that could understand that. And these people down here had never been used to that, lived up there, and they didn't want to leave their homes and go out, and it took a while, you know, but I didn't know somebody, and he worked pretty bad, he'd go up there and get work, and I don't know, they just had to, they went to the employment office, they had a regular employment office up there, in Spokane that they dealt with all the time, see. Whenever they needed men, they'd just say, send us so many. I know one time they sent in thirty men there, and they were real short, they'd been a bunch of them, well that was after the trouble was in the beginning to get straightened up, they sent for thirty men, and they came in there, had made a pretty tough proposition on me, because I had to keep track of them, I was clerking at the time, and I had to keep track of them, I had to actually have the names, you know, and they worked different prices, so much for hours, this kind of work, so much for hours, and other kind of work, and such as that, and maybe they'd work a while, and then take them off, and then the foreman didn't tell me when to change the man, well then I was in trouble, because at the end of the month I wouldn't have the right time for that man, see, I'd had him short changed or something, maybe, but anyway, they brought in thirty men there one time, there was a little incident there, it was kind of funny, I thought it was, so I had to take down all their names, and they come in, some of them fellas were so kind of gruff, and I don't know what it is, it didn't look like a very good bunch of fellas to me, but anyway, I'd go out around, trip it to where they were working to see how they were placed, what jobs and such, and I'd check their names, I didn't know all the names, you know, couldn't separate them, you know, to bring thirty men in there at one time, and I'd ask them their names again to be sure I had them lined out right, I'd come to one fellow there, and he says, I told him my name would not come in, ha, I hate that, and I didn't like it very well, I thought he'd ought to know that I couldn't get everybody straightened out

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: and I said, I explained to him what good I said, and he said, you know, thirty of you fellas come in here at one time, and I said, it's pretty hard for me, I've let you down, I know what you're doing, what you're drawing, I don't know, and he told me, I didn't forget it, I figured he wouldn't last long, I was pretty sure I had his number, and what kind of man he was, so finally, sure enough, he come in, so I said, oh boy, you're going to tell me your name once more, whether you like it or not, if you get a check, and he come in, he asked for his time, and I said, just crank it, and I said, what's your name, just crank it, he was like a sat, and he answered me real meek that time, ha ha ha, I felt better, I got my revenge, ha ha ha, but, they come, you get a bunch like that in just a little while, I don't like to tell them, it's called short staked men, sometimes they work a few days, and just get money enough to make it on the next camp, or maybe bum a while, and then they have what's called long staked men, they'll stay a few months and make money before they go up Spokane, they're out and do it, so a little incident a fellow told me that one time, he was kind of cute, he was nice, hopefully, he wasn't a foreigner, he was, I believe a French Canadian, but he was known for that, about 60 years, he was bucking wood for when the donkeys saw it by hand, you know, they burnt wood, and he worked there for months, and then he'd go to Spokane, and he'd live like a millionaire, nobody knew what he did, real wealthy, he'd go to a hotel, you know, and he'd get a taxi every place he went, and he'd tell a little joke on himself, and he said, he wanted to go someplace, and he didn't know where it was, and he said, he said he was too high toned when he lived in town to ask about it, he says, there was a taxi there, he says, I just jumped in and gave him the address, he said, told him where to take me, he says, they just whirled around the corner and stopped and let me out, he'd just tell that and laugh, you know, he's a good sport, you know, joke was on him, we didn't know nothing about it, he hadn't told us, but he had to tell us it was too funny, but those fellows were like that, you know, they'd go up there and spend everybody money they had just a little while, and back they come, and they're satisfied to work again for a while, and they'd nearly always get to work, they knew they had to put up with so much of that, you know, keep men. So they would hire through Spokane mostly, and I... Yes, through the employment office of Spokane, a fellow by the name of Featherstone would run the employment office up there, and they'd send up there for men, that was the worst deal I ever got into, bringing 30 men in there for me to keep track of at the same, at one time, usually, you know, just two or three men come in, or three or four, or something, just crazy to walk in, ask for a job, and those fellows, you'd get lined out, you'd maybe remember where the boss sent them, but sending a bunch like that, you couldn't hardly keep track of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was during the strikes?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, I believe it was, if I remember, I think this was after that, when they was beginning to get men again, and then they'd begin to see they'd lost all their old timers, and that was them. Now, they weren't so much men like I was telling you about earlier, you know, but they talked this little only in language, these were different types of people. I think these people were mostly citizens of this country, the majority of them, but they were not very high -grade citizens of the majority of them either. Generally, just the type that followed the logging camps, uneducated, crude, dirty, but they were good -hearted, and as a general rule, they were friendly and easy enough to get along with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how fast after the strike ended did conditions change, was it an immediate thing?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Pretty fast, because it probably seemed like quite a while then, but really it was fairly fast, because you see, it cost them a lot to shut down. They have to keep men hired all the time now, like monthly men, see I worked for them once, and the cooks worked for them once, the foremen worked for them once, and people like that, they're paying a steady salary all the time if they stay, see?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, those people all stayed, they didn't belong to those people, they stayed. And they had to pay them, and they also had to have watchmen, and at one time there they had three police, or sheriffs, whatever you call them in there, watching, and they were afraid during that IWW trouble that they would cause trouble, see?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And they had three of them hired, and the sheriff, he came over there, and at that time there was only 13 of us there, he deputized us all and gave us a permit to pack a gun, and I didn't take it very seriously, I didn't think there was gonna be any body, some of them thought there would, one fella's got to Moscow for a sheriff, and he worked as sheriff at Moscow for a long time, he was just scared to death out there watching the lights. He just thought somebody might shoot him, I'd hate to step out in front of that guy, it made a false move, I think he'd have shot him because he was scared. But I, oh, I was going with a girl up there at the time at Bovill, and I had a little .25 Colt automatic pistol, my brother -in -law bought one, we weren't afraid, we were just young fellas, and we bought them, and she found that pistol on me, and she accused me of being afraid to come and see her without packing a gun, and I didn't carry it anymore.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: So, I went back and forth there, it was about a ten mile walk, five miles into town, and then a ten mile walk and the boy went back, where the camp was, and I walked around with them fellas alone and everything, I had never had anybody offer to harm me, they talked to me and tried to get me to move out, but I told them a certain chance, I said I have to work, take care of my mother, I said I don't belong to your union, and you didn't tell me when to come here or anything, I said you can't tell me when to quit. And they'd be pretty nice to me, pretty reasonable, because I'd talk reasonable to them and give them my sides of it, and none of them ever bothered me at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel more closer to them or closer to the management people in the camps?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I don't know, I tell you, I had a high regard for the management. All those people, I wasn't high enough up to be one of them, I don't mean that, but the superintendent, when I first went to camp, he hired clerks and scalers, I worked directly under him, and he held a place for me after I'd been in the service there, and I came back and my clothes went to Preston, Idaho, they made a mistake on them, and he wrote down, I was going to go back to camp, but he wrote down and said he heard I was back, and thought probably I was going to go back. And he said I would like to have a job back, and said he had a place for me to come up. So I told him that my clothing was lost, and I didn't feel like buying new clothing for a while yet, I wanted to get my clothing and I'd be up as soon as I could get it, and it was ten days before I came up, and when I seen him there, he told me, he said I just apologized to me, he said I just kept that place open for you for ten days, and he said yesterday I had to send out a clerk, he said it was getting so balled up, no clerk there or anything, he said I had to send somebody out. And I told him, I said that's okay, I said I'll just go out to camp and go to work by the day, and you'll have an opening sometime or other, and I said whenever you have an opening for me, why don't you just let me know. He said no, you don't need to do that at all, he says I'll get an opening for you. So he told me to go out to camp two and scale, well he first told me to go down to I believe it was camp five or something, down to the helm or a dairy or some place, and scale, and the gypo was down there, and then the gypo as they called it, that was the people's contract, you see hauling with a thousand, they were just starting what they called the gypo at that time. And go down there, and I'd never scaled for any of them, of course which wouldn't have made much difference, but anyway next morning I was going to go down the train and I didn't know if I'd bought the ticket or not, and I said no, and he says if you haven't bought your ticket, I've changed my mind, he says I'll send the man at two down and let him scale for them, and he says you go out and take his place. So he gave me a note there and take it out to this man for him to go down to a camp tribe or whatever it was, I don't know, maybe I, anyway, down by helm or there, or some place down there, dairy, and scale, well he didn't like it, I didn't know that at the time, and he went on down to Bruce's, started to roll down there, but I didn't know that, but I went out, I kind of hated it after that, but I see I went out to two there, and scaling there then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is T .P. Jones, the superintendent that you're speaking of?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, yes, that's right.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well now he hired me to direct at that time, but you see, later on then, the more particular, see, and they would put a scaler and a clerk in camp, and then finally they got so that they had a scaler in the woods for a scaler after the saws, see everything began to go by the thousand, so much per thousand to the working man, see. Well they had to put a scaler in the woods to scale for the saws, and then they had to own the landing, because I was scaling the landing after they separated the scaling, that was the head scaling that was on the landing, and then they had a man to scale on the marion, they paid them for the thousand, but all he did was just barely scale it so they could pay the marion scalers, or the crews working on the marion. But they all, everything was working to the thousand for them, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get in disagreements with the men about your scale against their idea of what the scale should be on the log?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh yes, especially right in the start. There's a lot of tricks to all things, you know, all trade, you had to learn, I tried to be real honest, and I found out there were some ways you couldn't be too honest, I was honest enough in a way, but what I mean is there's little tricky ways that you had to adopt finally in order to get along with both sides. But they protect you, see, all that scaling, because if they didn't, they couldn't keep a scaler at all, you know, they'd run you off the job, the working men would, there'd always be some people that would holler and chase you off the job, and they had check scalers that would check on you pretty regularly, see, they'd come on and scale a hundred logs and compare it, you didn't know your logs was numbered, see, in your book. Well you could come there and scale, if you didn't even see them, and scale them, pick the numbers off, and end your logs, and then go to your books and tell them exactly what you gave them, see. So they had men checking you all the time, that protected you, you see, a lot, that was pretty good for you, see. But I scaled there for a number of years, and of course I was one of the, I don't know what I'd like to say, head scaler, I wasn't a head scaler, I was in camp, the landing scaler was the main scaler in camp, I was always the landing scaler, every place I was always the landing scaler. But, the tricky thing now, I found out the wood scaler, you see, the sawyers, they'd trick the scalers if they could, see. They'd ask the wood scaler how much he gave for a certain log there, and he'd tell them to be friendly, and I soon found out, when we got on the landing, they'd see that log maybe, and see me walk, oh see, won't you come and scale this log, I'd like to know how much scale it's in, we'd cut that as one, how much scale it's in. They're more or less oval as a rule, some are perfectly round, and another thing, your scale rule goes by inches, and there are two tricky ways of scaling a log, you may scale, if a log was perfectly round it wouldn't make any difference how you lay your rule on it. But, you can take and measure the narrow way, and the wide way of the log, and give the average between, but as it breaks on inches sometimes it isn't divisible, see. And you have to give or take just a little bit, well now, one scaler will give and the other will take, or maybe you'll give on this and take on the next one, and so on, see.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well there's going to be a little variation there, well the man who's working doesn't realize that. And another way you can go up and lay, one of the fastest ways, and probably as good as any, is just as you step up to a log, lay your rule on diagonal, your log is going to lay on its flat side, you see. And lay your rule on about a 45 degree angle there, and that'll catch the average, see that's the way I use the scaling.
