But anyway, I found out if there was a question on it, when I scaled this log, and I knew that it was he's checking on, trying to check on it, see, if there was two figures you could give it, I'd always give it the low figure, myself. Because I didn't care what he thought about me, I wasn't scaling for him, you see. And I didn't want to hurt the man that was scaling for him, because I figured that was an honest man, see, and I didn't want to get him in trouble. Well then, if there was a doubt in it, you see, and I gave the low one, then he'd be sad, see, his scaler gave the same one, you'd think that's fine, we'll give it. But if his scaler gave the other one, you see, he'd think, well I'm glad he's scaled for me, I wouldn't want that last scaler to scale for me. Well, you see, it's just a little trick to help one another out and to keep people out of trouble, there's nothing dishonest about it or anything like that, really. But I'll tell you something that's really kind of funny, it always was, I felt a little guilty about, and I didn't lie to the man. I never lied to him, I always squirmed around, somehow or other, but he had a fire, and the logs laid across, and everything, where two logs crossed one another, it burned them almost in two, sometimes it did burn them in two. And they always told us that they couldn't handle less than an eight foot log on the carriage down there, they couldn't handle it, see, if a log was under eight feet it was no good to cull it. Well, it was a funny thing, it happened to be a neighbor of mine at that time, poor guy's dead now, and his brother's dead, there's none of them left around, I probably wouldn't say anything about it now, but anyway, he was skinning by the thousand, and, well they told us sometimes not to cull a log, them blackened logs, you see, it was hard to tell if, not to write cull on it, if it had anything in it at all, just to double audit in your book, not give it anything, because they said if you did, they'd start leaving good logs in the woods, see. So we had to be a little bit sneaky about that, but anyway, this poor guy, he brought in a log, nice log, pretty one, it had laid across another log exactly in the middle, a sixteen foot log, see, and it burnt this log so near into it just barely it would hold it together for him to pull it down there. Well, it had run two feet out of the middle of a sixteen foot log, that left two seven foot chunks, see, we couldn't use them on the carriage. Well now if I'd have told him that that log was a cull, as solid as it was, he would have been mad at me as long as he lived, see, probably, and of course I was working under orders until we had our, we had to do these things, so I told him his name was Frank, not to say his last name, but I said, and I said, now Frank, I says, there's only two feet of that log ruined, I said that's a perfectly good log, except just two feet of it ruined there in the middle, and he says that's right, that's right, that's all that's ruined to it, that's all I said, I didn't lie to the man, but you see in my book I cull it.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: What time he gets his hundred logs and figures them up, he doesn't know the log was culled or anything about it, see, but now I'd have made an enemy if I'd explained that to him, don't you see, what I did is I'd have made an enemy, I didn't lie to the man, in a way I left the wrong impression, but you have to do some of them things sometimes, you see, just for everybody's sake, but then I had one fellow one time, he cut a bunch of, another Sawyer, I was scaling for Sawyer at that time, sometimes I scaled for both a little bit, and he said that, oh, he said that had to be more than that, he asked me what the scale was, see, I scaled there at the day he was cut there, and he was living, oh it had to be more than that, I said it isn't, I know it's got to be more than that, he says I've scaled some myself, I says I know better than that, and well, I didn't have to do anything, but it made me mad, and I could have just went back to camp and said nothing, he just probably wouldn't have quit, but anyway, I somehow or other thought that'll never clear me, and I asked him, you know how to scale, and he said yes, and I pinned him down on it, just how he scaled, and I told him, I said now, I'm gonna let you scale them logs, I want you to go out there and scale them and see what you get, I want you to show me what you get out of them, and I told him I want you to scale them the same rules that I do, I said you scale inside that bark, the wood only, that's the way we have to scale them, and I says if there's anything wrong with it, you deduct a little bit on it, and I says you come back and see, and while he was in there, I was talking to his partner, they were sitting there waiting for him, and his partner says he gets me into trouble every place we go, he says I'm gonna have to quit him, and says, and his partner made him mad and embarrassed him too, see, and he says he gets me in trouble every place we go, pretty soon he came back, he didn't want to say anything, I said how'd you come out?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he says, wasn't as much as I thought it was, I thought there was a lot more than that, they gave me the rule, he wouldn't even say, he wouldn't even tell me how he came out, I'd given him all I dared to, no money in my pocket to try to cheat him, another thing I liked about T .P. Jones, the wooden superintendent, I went to learn a man to scale one time, he sent a new man and he told me to teach him to scale, and he says, you tell him if there's a doubt, give it to the working man, he says there shouldn't be many doubts, but tell him if there's a doubt, you tell him to give it to the working man, now that's what the superintendent told me to tell him when I learned him, but yet they thought that they was getting gypped, don't you see, well I certainly had no money in my pocket to chip anybody, I didn't get it anymore, I was paid by the month, but some of them think you're that way, you know, they think you're gonna get something out of it, but one time, at a camp up the Elk River, the wood scalers up there, I believe it was, what was it, six thousand or something like that, anyway the principal will show you if I'm wrong, six thousand or a million of them in the woods, and they got them when there was only three, and they found out that these were just young kids, they scaled them, they found out that the sorters had been slipping them a few dollars and they'd been doubling their scale, so then that's what started making us, and happened to number logs, numbers, tumps and everything else, so they could check in the woods, they didn't used to check in the woods before that, all they did was fire them kids, but the rest of us that stayed had to pay for them, we had to do a lot of extra work just because they were crooks, did you find that you had to use some judgement a great deal in scaling and judging logs?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: On defective logs, yes, that's right, but the sound logs, no, there was not much judgement to use on that, you tried to give the average of whatever it was, give and take, if you thought you were a little low on each one, one to the next one, you give it, and no there wasn't much there, you'd scale, we suppose, I understood, I never did know for sure, keep it in five percent, but I know there's always less than that, sometimes it's one in a fraction percent of the checkskiller, and things in two and three, that was one in a fraction, it would be a very close scale, but on defective logs, now there's methods on that sometimes, I like, they figure, I don't know what they figure now, and they have government scalers and things, I might be contradicted on it, but all I can say is what we did, and under our instructions, now we had a government scaler there that checked on us a while, and he scaled and him and I scaled together down here on the first log that they ever hold apart, he picked me out to scale with him, and he told me that they figured on a rotten log, if you had a log that's rotten in the middle, you'd tell them the knots, see, on many and much timber, if the knots shows rot, they're going to be rotten in the tree, the log, and he said if a knot showed, rotten, and it was the log was found on both ends, that the rot would go twice as fast down the log tree as it would go up the tree, he says maybe this rot, for instance, would go up the tree two feet, but it would go down it four feet, it would probably be six feet in there, there was damage for that rot, and that was one way of telling like that, and a stump rot, you get a little familiar with them, there's what they call a stump rot, there would be a hole maybe when they're found, and they'd follow through right, and a stump, a hole or a rotten place, and those as a rule on a small buttered tree don't usually go very far, that's usually on a pine tree, yellow pine, they don't go very far, but you figure, well if maybe that goes in about four feet, why an average of about two feet would be a hole about so big, and you figure about how much it would take out, but now on others, what they call windshakes, sometimes the heart is a solid piece itself, and then the outside shell of that is a solid piece, but the two are not delighted, they'll fall apart when they go in the carriage, well they had a method they call a rule of thumb there that they used on that, I can't tell you anymore, just what it is, they measured this size and added so much to it and so on and so forth, I didn't know but I can't remember, and it varied with the size of your log, and that was a hard one to figure out, and you might vary quite a little, but you had all these methods until you had to vary a little but not too far,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did each species get scaled by a separate...

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: yes, each species, now I'll have to change that just a little bit, all the main species were separate, and then they took, what was it, two or three species that they didn't have hardly any of, low grade like white fur and larch, and I mean, I can't think of the name of the stuff, not larch, larch is tamarack, used to go with tamarack, and then it changed to larch, it changed the name of the species, but hemlock, well maybe white fur and hemlock and you'd put them both, and some place where there's no seed hardly, maybe mixed at all, and you put it as mixed, but you see you'd have your pine, and you'd have your red fur, Douglas fur on the coast, but they say it's the same thing really, and your tamarack we used to call it, and then it changed the books to larch, and then you'd have your mixture, you'd have about four things, and then you kept the length of your logs separate too, the scale of your 12 foot logs, and the 14, 16, 18, and 20's, in each timber, kept each timber and each length in each timber separate, see, and our old method was scaled on cards, if it was rainy, you'd put oil on the cards sometimes, or it's pretty hard to write on if there were any, and then when you had night we'd copy them into our regular scale books.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were cutting all the timber in most places that the operation was going on, and the man was supposed to take the tree regardless of what species it was?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, yes, that's right, of course this is the company, with all the different camps, if they wanted to get them together they could, you know, or anything, yes, they cut all species, it's too expensive to go through it once for one kind, and then back again, they had to, they took everything as they went through, and everything large enough, although one time they did go back on the scale of the camp where the little things were too small, they didn't reach the first number on the scale, it was hardly a second growth they thought it, and white pine, but I think maybe that had been taken clean, maybe it had been left there a few years, and then it reloaded a little bit, but how do you do that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the men feel about that? Was there any choice, I mean, was there much reluctance about having to log inferior species of trees when there was some good stuff?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, as far as quality and value was concerned, it didn't make any difference to them, they were paid by the thousand, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it didn't matter?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, there's absolutely no difference to the working men, see, they were paid by the thousand no matter what it was, see. They had maybe preferences for some, some timbers were limmering others, some was harder to limb and not, and you had to start with why they had to limb them and everything, you see. And any more, you know, when they put them out for these year, cats, you see, logging nowadays, they drag them down through the woods in time they handle them, manhandle them and everything, lots of times you see them come down on them and things like that, they didn't used to do that, but most of them limbs break off. But they got these little chainsaws, you know, and nothing to it, they used to be a good chop for an hour on some of them big limbs almost before you could get one more. Some of them were huge in the big timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I read in a book about Weyerhaeuser and the opinion of these men who were reviewing their logging history that they didn't do as a corporation all that well with the logging of potlatch forests. And the reason that was given was that they had clear cut and taken inferior stands of timber rather than concentrating on the prime timber because they believed that they should take it all, but that when you finally got done with all the costs, it wasn't worth it with some of these grades of timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any opinion about that?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I really don't know, but if they can't afford to take out a low grade of tree when they're at it, I don't see how they can come back and re -log it and take it out. That wouldn't sound sensible to me. They might not come out very good on some of these, but now then the low grade timbers, they used to have like white fir and things that was almost worthless and stink and everything else. Now then they're using it for a lot of things. Right on the market you pay a big price for it. But I don't know how well it would pay to go back if it was left and they damaged a lot of that stuff with logging anyway. And to go back and the wind falls down and everything else, and go back and re -log it to try to get that stuff, that doesn't look like good judgment to me. They had this clear cutting, I don't go much on that. I was back in some very off country, beautiful country there of green timber. And I was back here a year or so ago and the road was all through and there'd been a camp back there and the mountains were just bare and timber laying in every direction where everything had been falling. I don't know, I can't see that. Maybe smarter people than me got it figured out, but I read some articles here lately where some of the other people don't think that paid either to clear cut it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that different than the way they used to do it?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh yes, they didn't used to do that. They took all the mercantile timber out right while they was at it. If they had any sale for it all, they'd take all types out, see, all varieties. But this clear cutting, they call it now, they just go and slice everything that's left, everything that's on the ground standing, they slice it all. Just leave a mountain bare and I guess they figure on burning and reseeding or something, but I don't know, it certainly destroys a beautiful country. And it's a terrible looking thing to go back and look and see all that stuff laying flat on the mountains that once were beautiful green timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what was left after they used to cut?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well there would be trees that were too small, too long, you see all the trees and the trees that were too small. It seems to me like, of course maybe there was nothing left to seed, I don't know if there would have been enough little tiny ones to reseed it, thick enough for now, but maybe they could have scattered some seeds in there, maybe the squirrels, they used to do the reseeding. Maybe they didn't stay in there, I don't know if there would be any birds, any nuts for them to get a half sure of the logwood, maybe some on the trees that were worthless to last.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the slash burned afterwards?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I don't know too much about that, some of that was still up there when I was in that. That's the first and all that I ever seen, I'd heard of it, but there was acres.
